data.title: Trump calls Supreme Court's tariff ruling 'deeply disappointing'
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday criticized the Supreme Court's decision striking down his sweeping tariffs, calling the ruling "deeply disappointing."

Speaking at the White House, Trump said he was "absolutely ashamed" of Supreme Court members for "not having the courage to do what's right for our country," hours after SCOTUS ruled 6-3 that he exceeded his authority under an emergency powers law by imposing broad tariffs without clear congressional backing, 

Meanwhile, the president vowed to pursue alternative paths to advance his trade agenda. 

This is a developing story. Check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: KKR mulls sale of unit BMC Helix - report
data.description:
KKR & Co. (KKR) is considering a sale of its BMC Helix unit, people familiar with the matter told Reuters.

The New York-based firm has reportedly secured initial bids from private equity firms and corporate buyers.

BMC Helix generates about $150M in EBITDA and ~$750M in annual recurring revenue, the sources reportedly said.

The sale could fetch as much as $1.5B, according to the people, who requested anonymity while discussing non-public matters.

Investment bankers at Jefferies are said to be advising BMC Helix on the sale.

KKR declined to comment on the potential transaction.

data.title: Wall Street moves higher as market participants assess GDP and PCE data
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages move higher on Friday as investors react to the PCE report, which came in hotter than expected.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.5%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.45%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.1%. 

From a sector vantage point, six of the S&P 500’s 11 segments were in positive trading territory, with communication services at the top. Going the other way, energy has struggled the most so far. 

Treasury yields inched higher as the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up by 2 basis points to 3.48%, and the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved up by 2 basis points to 4.09%. 

On the economic calendar, PCE inflation ticked up in December and came in higher than the consensus. The Q4 U.S. GDP (first estimate) came in at +1.4%, annual rate, vs. +2.8% consensus and +4.4% in the prior quarter.

“Friday's GDP for the fourth quarter was much lower-than-expected, but it was still positive and shows that the economy expanded at respectable clip during the final few months of 2025 even as fears about the labor market started to intensify. We expect a strong year of economic growth in 2026, driven by business investment, consumer spending and fading trade headwinds,” Rick Gardner, chief investment officer of RGA Investments noted.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Corning Incorporated (GLW) climbed 6.3%, and shares of Intel (INTC) came down by 2.4%.

data.title: Celsius Holdings rallies after strong growth potential update
data.description:
Celsius Holdings (CELH) rallied on Friday after its presentation at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York Conference caught the eye of investors. Notably, the beverage company expects its distribution footprint to continue growing, as the Celsius and Alani Nu brands continue to show momentum. 

Management said it sees a long runway of double‑digit top‑line growth, driven by category tailwinds, broader distribution, and an expanded multi‑brand energy platform anchored by the key PepsiCo (PEP) partnership. Structural shifts by consumers toward zero-sugar, functional products are seen as a tailwind. Consumption trends are also working in Celsius' (CELH) favor, with more consumers using energy drinks across social, meal, and fitness moments.

Looking ahead, convenience stores are considered a significant white‑space opportunity, with c‑stores representing ~60% of energy sales. Celsius (CELH) signaled a significant opportunity to increase its share of space and facings in the channel.Shares of Celsius Holdings (CELH) were up 8.0% in early afternoon trading to reverse a four-week downtrend.

data.title: Employers signals 10% premium growth target for new excess workers’ comp product while advancing AI-driven efficiency
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Employers Holdings, Inc. (EIG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Katherine Antonello began by addressing the elevated frequency of California cumulative trauma (CT) claims, noting "To be clear, this remains a California-specific issue. Claim frequency in our other states and within non-CT claims in California continues to trend favorably." She emphasized that Employers implemented rate increases and tightened underwriting restrictions in California and other regions, while adding, "these actions... are also likely to reduce written premium in 2026."
  • Antonello highlighted that the Small Commercial segment maintained strong retention rates throughout 2025, attributing this to investments in automation and ease of use. She confirmed that the fourth quarter full actuarial assessment required no additional reserve strengthening, and an independent actuarial firm validated that carried reserves were within reasonable ranges.
  • A new excess workers’ compensation product was introduced, described as "a strategic expansion of our capabilities," with Antonello reporting "the early market response has been strong, and we expect this product will deepen our distribution partner relationships while expanding our addressable market."
  • $215 million was returned to shareholders via share repurchases and dividends in 2025. Antonello stated, "Along with our operational performance, these actions increased our book value per share, including the deferred gain by 11% to $51.31."
  • CFO Michael Pedraja reported, "Gross premiums written were $156.8 million compared to $176.3 million for the prior year quarter, a decrease of 11% due primarily to a decrease in new business writings and lower final audit premiums, partially offset by higher renewal business premium."
  • Pedraja added, "Our losses in LAE were $134.4 million versus $113.2 million a year ago, an increase of 18.7% due primarily to an increase in the accident year 2025 selected loss and LAE ratio and the absence of favorable developments in the fourth quarter of this year."
  • Pedraja detailed the investment portfolio rebalancing, noting, "The investment rebalancing accomplished several goals, including reducing our equity investments to target allocation, increasing our overall investment portfolio yield by a net 40 basis points, extracting an estimated net present value gain of $16 million and reducing our required capital."

Outlook

  • Management expects a reduction in written premium in 2026 as a result of tightened underwriting and pricing actions in California. Antonello noted, "While we're confident that these California pricing and underwriting actions... will strengthen our underwriting profitability, they are also likely to reduce written premium in 2026."
  • The company anticipates further improvement in the expense ratio, driven by ongoing AI initiatives. Antonello said, "We believe it will continue to decline with our enterprise-wide deployment of AI."
  • Employers is targeting the new excess workers’ compensation product to be a "meaningful top line revenue growth driver" over the next several years, aiming for it to constitute "10% of our overall written premium over the next 4 to 7 years."

Financial Results

  • Gross premiums written for the quarter were $156.8 million. Losses and loss adjustment expenses totaled $134.4 million. Net investment income reached $31.4 million, attributed to private equity investment returns and a higher book yield on the fixed income portfolio.
  • After a portfolio rebalancing, the weighted average book yield increased to 4.9%. The after-tax realized loss on fixed income sales was $40 million, reducing net income and adjusted book value per share for the quarter.
  • Adjusted net income, excluding realized/unrealized investment gains and losses and LPT deferred gain amortization, was $14.5 million for the quarter.
  • Employers repurchased almost 2.4 million shares at an average price of $40.94 per share in Q4, and continued buybacks into early 2026.

Q&A

  • Mark Hughes, Truist: Asked about the trajectory of CT claims in California. Antonello responded, "We are seeing throughout 2025 that the acceleration of the frequency that we saw sort of in early '25 and throughout 2024... has flattened quite a bit. So that has been good news."
  • Hughes questioned the competitive dynamic in California. Antonello explained, "I would characterize the California market as hardening. Generally speaking, though, I'd say across the country, the environment is still fairly competitive."
  • Hughes inquired about share buybacks for 2026. Antonello stated, "I do expect it will return to a normal level of repurchase authority in 2026, absent some change, but we're trying to be very opportunistic."
  • Hughes asked about further expense ratio improvement if top line is down. Antonello said, "We are hoping to still get improvement. As I mentioned, we have a lot of AI initiatives that are underway."
  • Karol Chmiel, Citizens: Queried about gross written premium in California. Antonello confirmed, "We're seeing lower new business writings in California, and we've selected some classes of business that we are exiting, not just in California, but countrywide."
  • Chmiel followed up on scaling the new excess workers’ comp product and future offerings. Antonello replied, "We do have other products in mind. We're not quite ready to announce those yet... we think over time that this could be a meaningful top line revenue growth driver for us as a company."
  • Unknown Analyst (Green Capital) probed on competitive advantages and target performance for excess workers’ comp. Antonello responded, "We do feel like we're going to be able to provide quotes in a faster manner because of the AI tool... relative to the guaranteed cost business... the excess comp space, while it's a bit, what I would say, lumpier, overall, we feel like it's going to perform in the mid-80s in terms of a combined ratio."
  • When asked about long-term scale, Antonello said, "We would love to see this be 10% of our overall written premium over the next 4 to 7 years."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly pressed on the trajectory of CT claims, competitive positioning, and the scaling of new products, reflecting a slightly cautious tone with a focus on risk management and future growth drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, particularly in prepared remarks. Antonello stated, "We're absolutely confident in the path that we're on." During Q&A, management was deliberate and transparent, but used caveats such as "not ready to claim victory yet" when discussing trends, indicating a measured approach.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, analysts showed continued concern about California CT claims, while management’s confidence appeared more grounded, emphasizing validation of reserves by external actuaries and the flattening of adverse claim trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q3, management discussed a significant reserve strengthening due to California CT claims and announced the initial development of the excess workers’ comp product. In Q4, management confirmed no additional reserve strengthening was needed and reported validation from an independent actuary.
  • The Q4 call emphasized execution: product launch for excess workers’ comp (now accepting submissions), expanded AI initiatives, and a completed recapitalization plan. Guidance now signals a likely reduction in written premium due to tighter underwriting and pricing in California, whereas Q3 focused on underwriting margin over growth.
  • Analysts' questions in both quarters focused on CT claim trends, competitive dynamics, and capital deployment, but in Q4, attention shifted toward the scaling and expected impact of the new product.
  • Management’s tone evolved from caution and defensive explanations in Q3 to a more forward-looking, confident stance in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated frequency of California CT claims remains a business risk, though management noted a flattening of the trend.
  • Management acknowledged that tightened underwriting and pricing may reduce written premium in 2026.
  • The after-tax realized loss of $40 million from fixed income sales impacted net income and adjusted book value per share in the quarter.
  • Competitive pressure in California persists, with management choosing to exit certain classes and geographies where pricing margins are too thin.
  • The performance of the new excess workers’ comp product and its integration into the portfolio presents execution risks, though early indications are positive.

Final Takeaway

Employers Holdings, Inc. is executing a disciplined strategy to address California CT claim pressures through targeted pricing, underwriting, and investment in automation, while simultaneously launching a new excess workers’ compensation product that is expected to drive up to 10% of written premium over the next 4 to 7 years. The company’s expense ratio continues to improve, underpinned by enterprise-wide AI adoption, and reserves have been validated by independent actuaries. Management remains confident in its capital position and shareholder return strategy, as evidenced by share repurchases and dividends, while signaling a cautious approach to premium growth in 2026 amid ongoing market challenges in California.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil States outlines $680M–$700M 2026 revenue target as offshore and international mix rises to 77%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oil States International, Inc. (OIS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Cindy Taylor reported "strong fourth quarter results with adjusted EBITDA exceeding our guidance and quarterly cash flows from operations at historically high levels." Taylor announced that $50 million of cash flow from operations was used to retire an equivalent amount of convertible senior notes, and "our cash on hand exceeded outstanding debt by $15 million at year-end."
  • Taylor emphasized progress in "optimizing Oil States' business mix in favor of operations focused in the offshore and international markets." She noted, "77% of our revenues generated from offshore and international markets in the current quarter compared to 72% in the prior-year period."
  • Taylor detailed that the Offshore Manufactured Products segment had "revenues and adjusted segment EBITDA increasing 13% and 12% sequentially," and that backlog reached $435 million—the highest since March 2015—with a quarterly book-to-bill ratio of 1.3x. She cited "multiple new contracts and successfully deployed advanced offshore technologies," including the "first successful deployment of our Low Impact Workover Package" and a "record deployment" of the Merlin Deepsea Mineral Riser System.
  • CFO Lloyd Hajdik stated, "During the fourth quarter, we generated revenues of $178 million, up 8% sequentially from the third quarter and adjusted consolidated EBITDA of $23 million, representing a 9% sequential increase and at the top of the guided consolidated EBITDA range that we provided on our third quarter 2025 earnings call."
  • Hajdik reported a "net loss of $117 million or $2.04 per share, which included long-lived asset impairments, restructuring charges and valuation allowances established on U.S. deferred tax assets." He clarified, "Our adjusted net income totaled $8 million or $0.13 per share after excluding these charges."

Outlook

  • Taylor projected "2026 full year revenues to range between $680 million and $700 million and full year EBITDA to range between $90 million and $95 million, both metrics up meaningfully year-over-year."
  • For the first quarter of 2026, Taylor guided for "revenues in a range of $150 million to $155 million and EBITDA of $18 million to $19 million."
  • Cash flows from operations for 2026 are expected to be "in a range of $60 million to $65 million, down from 2025 due to expected working capital builds."
  • Taylor reinforced the company will continue "advancing differentiated technologies and services aligned with customer needs, driving margin durability, generating free cash flow and delivering long-term stockholder value creation."

Financial Results

  • Hajdik reported "revenues of $178 million, up 8% sequentially from the third quarter" and "adjusted consolidated EBITDA of $23 million, representing a 9% sequential increase."
  • A net loss of $117 million was recorded, including $112 million in noncash long-lived asset and inventory impairment charges in the Downhole Technologies segment.
  • Adjusted net income was $8 million or $0.13 per share after excluding these charges. For the full year, adjusted consolidated EBITDA was $83 million, adjusted net income $22 million, and adjusted EPS $0.37 per share.
  • Cash flow from operations reached $50 million in the quarter, and free cash flow for the full year was $94 million.
  • Segment performance included Offshore Manufactured Products revenue of $123 million and adjusted EBITDA of $25 million (20% margin), Completion and Production Services revenue of $23 million and adjusted EBITDA of $7 million (32% margin), and Downhole Technologies revenue of $32 million and adjusted EBITDA of $1.3 million.
  • Hajdik stated, "We intend to use U.S. cash on hand and borrowings under our new credit agreement to retire these notes on or before their maturity on April 1, 2026."

Q&A

  • Stephen Gengaro, Stifel: Asked whether the Completion and Production restructuring is now fully reflected in Q4 revenue. Taylor confirmed, "the answer to that is yes," highlighting ongoing mitigation of exit costs and pointing to future margin improvement. Hajdik shared, "in the fourth quarter, there was about $1 million of revenues from the exited operations."
  • Gengaro questioned backlog margin improvements. Taylor indicated that consistent margin improvement is targeted, with mix and facility utilization being key factors, and stated, "We are targeting fairly consistent margins on a blended basis for 2026, but on strong revenues."
  • Gengaro asked if there was anything unusual in the 32% C&P margin. Taylor responded, "we may have had a one-off facility or equipment sale gains...but we are comfortable with EBITDA margins for that business in kind of the 30% to...34% range."
  • James Rollyson, Raymond James: Inquired about the sustainability of backlog growth. Taylor noted the addition of new products and emphasized the expanded manufacturing footprint, saying, "from a facility and manufacturing footprint perspective, we're actually more capable than we were a decade ago."
  • Rollyson asked about cash flow deployment post-debt repayment. Hajdik said, "After we pay the debt off, we should be opportunistic in buying back more shares and obviously have more optionality to look at M&A as a result."
  • Rollyson requested details on military products. Taylor described these as "an adaptation, honestly, of some of our FlexJoint technology...used in sound and vibration dampening applications on submarines."
  • Joshua Jayne, Daniel Energy Partners: Asked about offshore geographic opportunities. Taylor cited "Brazil has been and will continue to be a very strong base," and highlighted expansion in Southeast Asia and emerging activity in West Africa.
  • Jayne followed up on the offshore cycle. Taylor stated, "we are clearly underinvesting for the long term If you believe in the ultimate demand for crude oil." She believes the company is positioned for a multi-year upcycle.
  • Jayne queried tariff impact. Taylor detailed, "the tariff rate went from 25% to 98% about midyear...part of our improvement in kind of our perforating operation was delayed because of tariffs."
  • Gengaro asked about M&A focus. Taylor responded, "what we're looking for is just differentiated...technology-differentiated opportunities, whether that's an organic investment or through M&A."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was positive, with questions focusing on sustainability of margin improvements, backlog quality, cash flow deployment, and geographic expansion, and comments such as, "Congrats on all the progress."
  • Management tone during prepared remarks was confident and focused, using phrases like, "we are well positioned" and "we are confident in our ability to continue the momentum achieved in 2025." During Q&A, management maintained a constructive and transparent demeanor, directly addressing concerns and providing detailed explanations.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained positive but probed deeper into execution, margin durability, and capital allocation. Management's tone was more assertive about strategic progress and future prospects, reflecting higher confidence following strong results.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to more confident, upwardly revised targets for 2026 revenues and EBITDA.
  • Strategic focus sharpened further toward offshore and international markets, with the offshore/international mix rising to 77% of revenues from 75% in Q3.
  • Analysts increased focus on backlog quality, margin sustainability, and capital deployment as the company deleveraged and improved cash flows.
  • Key metrics improved: consolidated revenues, adjusted EBITDA, offshore product backlog, and cash flows all rose quarter-over-quarter. Impairment charges and restructuring costs related to U.S. land exits were more prominent in Q4.
  • Management confidence increased, emphasizing successful execution of portfolio optimization, high levels of cash generation, and a strong balance sheet. Analyst sentiment remained positive, with more attention paid to long-term strategy and capital allocation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Taylor acknowledged subdued U.S. land activity, with ongoing restructuring and asset sales expected to continue reducing exposure and costs.
  • Taylor flagged tariffs as a headwind, especially in the Downhole Technologies segment, stating, "the tariff rate went from 25% to 98% about midyear...part of our improvement...was delayed because of tariffs."
  • Ongoing property taxes, insurance, and utilities on exited facilities will persist until assets are monetized, with total assets held for sale approximating $17 million.
  • Taylor noted that while backlog strength supports earnings visibility, mix and timing of contract awards could affect near-term margin realization and cash flow.

Final Takeaway

Oil States International enters 2026 with a sharpened focus on offshore and international growth, a strengthened balance sheet, and a decade-high backlog. The company expects meaningful revenue and EBITDA growth, driven by differentiated technologies, disciplined execution, and robust cash generation. Management remains committed to enhancing shareholder returns through opportunistic share repurchases, prudent capital allocation, and continued expansion in high-margin, long-cycle markets while managing restructuring risks and navigating tariff-related headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DHT secures second VLCC this week at premium rate
data.description:
DHT Holdings (DHT) +0.9% in Friday's trading, reaching as high as $17.14 for the tanker company's best intraday level in nearly 15 years, after locking in its second high-paying time charter this week.

DHT (DHT) said late Thursday it has entered into a one-year time charter agreement with an unnamed global energy company at $94K/day for the very large crude carrier DHT Taiga, expected to begin in March.

The rate comes in higher than the $90K/day deal announced earlier this week with another unnamed company for the VLCC DHT Opal, expected to begin this month; both vessels were built in 2012.

The latest deal also edged past a recently reported fixture by Okeanis Eco Tankers (ECO), which fixed the 2016-built VLCC Nissos Nikouria at $91,140/day for one year, according to Splash247.com.

data.title: Current US dollar decline is modest compared to previous historical selloffs
data.description:
The U.S. dollar’s (DXY) recent decline—down 8.1% from a year ago—is nothing companies with historical selloffs, and remains overvalued on the most objective measures, according to Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at Corpay Currency Research.

Schamotta added that after a near-term hawkish repricing, the dollar (DXY) could even fall a lot farther if the policy direction doesn’t change.

 

 

 

The inflation adjustment dollar (DXY) has only declined about 10%, compared to the fourth quarter 2024 high, which was driven up by the initial Trump trade policy.

“The inflation-adjusted dollar is more or less back where it was in 2023 and most of 2024—and that is still a very strong dollar,” said Brad Setser, economist and former Counselor to the U.S. Trade Representative.

Other crises have led the U.S. dollar to decline way further. For example, during the financial crisis, the U.S. dollar dropped -28% from its high back then.

Year-to-date, the U.S. dollar index (DXY) is -0.4%, and from a month ago, it is -1%

data.title: Corning rises as UBS sees stronger signals from hyperscaler demand
data.description:
Corning (GLW) shares rose 6.5% in midday trading on Friday as UBS said it has seen stronger demand signals from the major hyperscalers. 

“This is driven by recent 30-50% revisions in capex spending by major hyperscalers,” analyst Joshua Spector wrote in a note to clients. “We expect this to mean more fiber deployment within data centers and further lift for GLW's Optical sales.” 

Spector, who has a Buy rating on Corning, upped his price target to $160 from $125 and raised his earnings estimates based on the strong demand.

As such, he now expects sales to grow at a compound annual rate of 21% through 2028 and adjusted earnings per share at a 30% clip.

“We think there is potential for further revisions up post NVDA update later this week, and we don't think this is the last capex raise for hyperscalers either,” Spector added. “Therefore we believe there is room to see further increases in GLW's fiber sales, which we forecast growing the Optical segment to 55% of GLW's net income by 2028.”

Spector also pointed out that there is likely more fiber capacity to come by 2028, as he pointed to the recent $6B deal with Meta (META) as a sign the demand is there. He also expects there will be deals with the other hyperscalers, which means Corning could win more market share. 

“These contracts are giving GLW visibility to longer-term demand growth, but also have the potential to bring in cash earlier to fund investments, in part de-risking investments in new capacity,” Spector explained. 

Additionally, it's unlikely that 2028 will be the year for peak sales, as there is likely to be higher-than-historical growth for fiber well into the middle of next decade, Spector added.

data.title: HomesToLife soars more than 40% amid tariff reset by the Supreme Court
data.description:
HomesToLife Ltd. (HTML) rallied more than 40% on Friday as investors locked in on the company as a clear beneficiary of the Supreme Court striking down the Trump Administration's sweeping tariff policies.

The global furniture company has two core divisions. A consumer retail division with direct operations in Singapore and Korea and a wholesale & trade division. HomesToLife (HTML) combines retail, distribution, and sourcing, supported by a diversified manufacturing network across China, Vietnam, and India.

Shares of HomesToLife (HTML) rose to as high as $2.58 on Friday after starting the day at $1.83. 

Last month, HomesToLife (HTLM) announced that it is investing $1M for a 10% fully diluted equity stake in Zeica Labs, a Singapore-based spatial-audio technology start-up behind the patented Super X-Fi immersive audio platform. The investment was made via wholly owned subsidiary HTL Marketing and said to position Zeica as HomesToLife's (HTLM) core innovation and technology partner.

Under the collaboration, Zeica will help integrate patented spatial-audio capabilities into selected upcoming furniture collections, with Singapore as the initial testbed before rolling out to other key markets. HomesToLife frames the deal as a strategic step to build next-generation smart home products and reinforce its positioning as a technology-forward furniture company, rather than as a purely financial investment.
